video_title: Fireside Chat with Sal Khan and Asst. Superintendent Phil Misecko

segments.text: Good morning, good afternoon, uh good
evening depending on where you are. I am
here with Dr. Phil Miko and Sal Khan. Um
we're here for a superintendent fireside
chat. Phil, you have agreed without
really knowing to kick off this series.
Uh S is going to be in conversation with
other superintendent later this month.
Uh if you happen to be at Spring Q
powered by Cali, S will be in
conversation with Amy Alzena from Cold
Spring and Dr. Dr. Ruth Paris of LA
County um with more to come. But let's
just get into it. S you built Khan
Academy from there was more to it than
just this but essentially a YouTube
channel into infrastructure that I don't
you probably heard this news cell but we
crossed over to 200 million learners
just yesterday the other day. So that's
pretty incredible. And you've spent the
last few years thinking about what AI
has to do with all of this. Phil, you're
in the superintendency. you know what it
costs politically, operationally to
actually move a to actually move a
district. So, I thought it'd be really
interesting to get you two in
conversation kind of leading in sort of
different directions kind of working on
the same thing. Um, my job in this
conversation is to try to just keep us
out of the clouds. If something gets too
abstract, I'm bringing it back.
>> No one's pointed at me.
>> Yeah. So, let's just get into it. Phil,
no warmup. Um, what is keeping you up
right now?
>> So, I just returned from a two-day
finance workshop in Indie. I'm uh I'm in
the state of Indiana, and what is really
worrying me right now is funds for
public education in our state. Um,
specifically in our district, we have uh
a really good highquality teachers and a
and a I feel like a really good thing
going. So, how do we continue to provide
that quality with less money every year?
Um, we're we're predicted to be 6.1
million shortfall over the next 3 years,
which is that that's not just a little
bit, that's a lot of money, uh, a lot of
loss to deal with. So, if if anything's
keeping me up, it's, you know, how do we
continue to provide what our kids and
our community expect and that we want to
give them with less money every year?
>> Phil, when what what would it actually
look like on, you know, Tuesday morning
if school year starts?
What what how are how are you thinking
about about this change in in real life?
You know, dollars missing, not having
what you need. What what could that look
like?
Well, I think I think as far as
you know from from my seat, I have to
listen to all the directors. I have to
listen to all the principles. We have to
take into consideration every single
person that we hire and is this
something that is necessary? Not just
the people, but also the curriculum,
what we use, what we purchase. I mean,
we've got to look at everything from
platforms, from programs, from adoption.
Um, you know, it goes into e even
transportation. I mean, do we continue
to transport kids back and forth to
school and we do a lot of uh a good job
at that, but there are districts around
us that have already hit a point where
they they can't afford to take kids um
go pick them up and bring them to
school. So, um I I we have a really good
CFO. we're in a good spot, but we don't
want to end up at two years from now.
Oh, oh my gosh, what do we do now? Um,
so those conversations and that planning
has to start. So, so nothing, you know,
pressing but definitely through
attrition. Okay, somebody retires, do we
really need to continue this? Okay, the
adoption was five years ago for K5
literacy. do we readopt or can we and
can we do you know continue without
spending another $350,000
on a on a literacy program. So those
types of things that we have to do
when you hear Phil describe that
pressure. What goes through your head?
>> Yeah. Well, I mean I deep empathy. It's
it's it's not an easy um you know we we
see obviously some dimensions of what
goes on in a district but um you know
when you but I think I think we have the
luxury of you know thinking about okay
we're how do we improve the academic
outcomes and how do we improve you know
whether it's the math the reading the
science scores etc etc which I think we
all agree are important but you know
even intellectually I knew some of the
things that you you said but to hear it
out loud and to think about weighing you
know, can you have school buses? How
does it affect all these other programs?
Um, yeah, it's just incredibly complex.
>> I wonder, S, do you have a sense sort of
for where edtech has historically kind
of misread what districts actually need?
>> Well, I I think it's both edtech and and
sometimes the districts um sometimes
misread. I think there is a uh I I I I I
think there's there's almost a natural
instinct and and we've fallen for it
sometimes too. Uh where there's a new
technology and you hear a lot of press
about it and it seems like other people
are using it to good effect because they
just got profiled in some newspaper or
something and then you feel pressured to
use it to do something about it. I think
edtech um does that. Um, and and and I
think districts sometimes do. We all
remember, you know, when I was a a kid,
I remember the computer labs that were
for the most part gathering dust in the
1980s. And, you know, I was probably one
of the few kids that that used it, but
those were expensive. Um, we all
remember when the tablets came out, iPad
came out, you know, there were some
districts that put a lot a lot of money,
like huge amounts of money in it, but
they really didn't have a a a strong use
case for it or or a theory of action.
And I think look AI um there's there's a
lot of that too right now. Uh there's uh
okay this seems modern. We're hearing
these promises that it can do this for
us, it can do that for us. Uh but I I
think I actually I think we're already
seeing this that you know that cycle has
passed and now people are back to
especially if they're facing budgetary
constraints. are saying, okay, what's
actually um improving the objectives
that we care about for for our students?
And you know, just speaking about AI in
particular, and actually I'll go go all
the way back to when how you introduced
me. Um, you know, most people do
associate Khan Academy back in the day
with videos because to some degree that
was like the the sexier thing, the thing
to notice, but from the beginning Khan
Academy was exercises that progress at
your pace. Um it was always this theory
that okay to learn something well you
have to get a little bit more
personalization fill in your gaps have
practice at your learning edge with
immediate feedback the way you would do
in anything you want to get better at
you know a sport or or or a musical
instrument and you know you fast forward
Aviv you know this uh you know this has
always been the focus of Khan Academy
the videos were just a a supplement to
that that kind of took on a life of
their own and they helped a lot of
people get to know about Khan Academy
they were very lightweight way to use
Khan Academy. But in a lot of ways, I
think AI is very similar to the videos.
Um I I think the story has been a AI and
we we have definitely also at Khan
Academy um leaned into that story, but
uh I think we have all appreciated that
it's not AI for AI's sake. You have to
take a step back and say what's the
objective you're trying to solve for.
Our primary objective at Khan Academy is
we want to improve academic outcomes as
measured by interim assessments,
summitive uh test states test scores. Um
that's number one uh goal. Um and then
you know number two and three goals if
we can make teachers lives easier, if we
can make administrators lives easier as
we do that uh then I think that's a good
thing. And you know what we've seen in
the first wave of our AI tools in Kigo
is
you know you can put it out there and
immediately there there were definitely
I would say 15 20% of students
immediately kind of figured out how to
use it and for the most part saw a lot
of benefit. A lot of folks know you know
you can't use Kamigo to cheat. There's a
lot of transparency to the district and
to the uh administrators and to the
teachers. But what we appreciated is it
I mean again we appreciate it it's the
learning is happening on all of our
efficacy studies not because of the AI
the learning is happening because
students are getting more pro more
practice at their zone of proximal
development teachers are in the loop no
matter whether you have AI videos
anything the teachers are the main
engagement mechanic they're the ones
that can hold the students accountable
the ones that they make sure they do the
work um they're the ones when the the
edte tool isn't enough uh that the the
teacher um does those in-person
interventions or helps motivate the
students. So anyway, I think uh not
technology for techn sake and it's got
to work as part of a human system. And I
think also from the district point of
view, you know, you can't just drop some
of this stuff in and think it's just all
going to solve all your problems. I
don't think anyone maybe feels that way.
But um it it there's real work, you
know, the tool is part of it, but
there's a lot of work uh to to make it
to make it work. And you know, uh, Phil
mentioned the the the dollar cost of
these things. And, you know, we're a
nonprofit. All of our R&D is funded by
philanthropy.
Our our tools are are are free generally
for districts that want support and
training, etc. We're charging them, you
know, on the order of $15 a per student
per year. So, that's real money,
especially when you hear about, you
know, kind of the financial situations
that happening throughout the district.
But, I would say the real investment is,
okay, is this one of the top three
priorities of the district? you know,
move forward, make sure there's training
support for teachers, um, etc., etc.
And, you know, even on the dollars, we
all we we we love to work with the
districts to figure out are there ways
that we can get local philanthropy,
corporations involved, state, other
sources. Um, we've so far been pretty
good at that.
>> Yeah. Um, Phil or S, just a few weeks
ago, we had Phil and his team um, come
do a live event for us where they spoke
about their a little more than half a
year now of partnering with us on the
re-imagined Khan Academy experience. And
so, they're they've been amazing pilot
partners. Um, so I was hoping Phil if
you could kind of recap for us. I think
it's super interesting where AI is
fitting into your academic goals right
now because you have really specific
examples. So I would love if you could
just speak to that.
>> Yeah. So, you know, as S was talking, I
it took me back to probably a handful of
years or so, 10 uh time goes fast, but
10 years ago, I was a middle school
principal and um we did something with
seemed crazy at the time, but we quit
giving homework. And we did that
specifically in math. We found that when
kids would go home and practice math
wrong, it took seven times the effort to
erase that bad practice and retach them.
and we were in a high poverty school. We
just didn't have time to do that. So, we
just shut down homework. So, we needed
to practice in front of teachers to
provide that immediate uh feedback and
and constructive criticism. And we found
that our our math kids grew a lot more.
I I feel like kids are expected to do
more and more at a younger age. we
necessarily don't have the the option
not to do have them practice at home. So
K migo Khan Academy what I what I think
that brings to the table is with K migo
kids don't practice math wrong. It
doesn't allow that allow them to do so.
It will prompt them answer questions.
It's it's a personal tutor that takes
that journey with them. Um, I I think
the same as writing. Writing is such a
difficult skill to teach, right? And I
was just reminded not too long ago that
we're six years away from the kids that
that were in COVID being out of the
school. So, we're still seeing that huge
gap in uh ability levels and kids that
are, you know, in in now in secondary uh
uh grades. But in in order to really
learn how to write well, you need
constructive feedback. And I look at,
you know, you you take a an English
teacher that has a 100 students and kids
write a paragraph. You you don't receive
that constructive feedback for a couple
weeks, the kids have already moved on to
the next assignment and it it's not as
meaningful. I think I heard a study,
somebody in this area did a study that
if you do not use some type of AI in in
con as a con writing coach. Um, kids get
typically one and a half times of
constructive feedback per assignment. If
you use AI, it bumps up to 22 and a half
times of constructive feedback over the
course of one year. I mean, if you're
talking about four lengthy writing
assignments, you go from what, six
iterations of feedback compared to over,
you know, 80, um, in the course of four,
five, six years, you're going to be a
different writer. I mean, you can't help
but grow. There are a lot of high
schools now asking for Grammarly and
things because they see the positive
effects of kids getting that immediate
feedback. So I I think the difficult
thing
[clears throat] um and and I thought
about this a lot. The difficult thing uh
for us is um Aviva I kind of forgot what
you asked now so I'm sorry.
>> Oh no that's okay. Just go ahead.
>> Well the difficult thing is we we have
uh Khan Academy this great platform. How
do we fit it into our curriculum and our
expectations? Right.
>> That was the question. And sometimes
sometimes the the difficulty is when
you're in a good district with good
teachers, they're doing good things and
they're getting good results, right? So
it it's not this, okay, everybody has to
use it for 30 minutes be because they're
things are going well with their kids.
So, so you have to find teachers that
are techy, that are not afraid of AI,
that need some additional, see the value
in some additional resources, good
additional resources, and and Sal
alluded to this. They're getting
bombarded by silver bullets. This is
going to fix everything in your
classroom, which is not the case. And
get some of those teachers to use it.
you see the results, they start sharing,
well, I learned checkpoints is a
formative assessment for our state
standardized test. When scores go up,
well, how did you how did you accomplish
that? And they start sharing that
information and then that starts growing
and moving across the district. Um,
sometimes probably like you guys, I I I
wish changes happened faster. Um, but
it's just not the way it works. change
is difficult for people and you have to
be uh very uh mindful. Um and you also
have to give some grace. I mean they
they'll come around if it's a it's a if
it's a good tool that's going to improve
student achievement. They will come
around.
Um, Phil,
uh, on the on the kind of getting
everyone on board thing, what is what is
happening when there's really good stuff
happening in one classroom, but it's not
happening at the district level? Like,
what's going on there? What would it
take to change that?
>> Well,
the leadership is so important, not not
only at the the central office, but also
at the building level. Everybody has to
value the change. I think we're at a
time right now and I read the brave new
world that's over uh Sal's shoulder. He
alluded in that book to uh AI will never
replace good teaching in a classroom.
Teachers you teachers are always going
to be valued. They're always going to be
needed. But I can tell you right now
when you look at the paper and you're
seeing, you know, JP Morgan is cutting
three, you know, tons of people because
AI is starting to do those little things
that they don't need people to do. Even
though teachers know that they're that
they're needed at the core, they start
having questions like, is this going to
take my place? So I think us constantly
uh reassuring teachers, you are the best
thing for our kids in the classroom. Now
what can we use to help you support your
kids to maximize growth? Um what can we
do to teaching right now is harder than
it's ever been. There's a wider range of
abilities in a classroom. Teachers are
expected to give individualized
instruction. They're expected to give
individualized feedback. They're
expected to tutoring. I think next year
there's some legislation in Indiana
where K8 we have to fit in 30 m excuse
me 30 minutes of RTI tier three
instruction outside of the regular
mandated classroom. So So I I I guess
that it's th this is what the goal is.
This is the vision. What can we do to
help you? And then if you have
trainings, we have e-learning days. You
get teachers to talk to other teachers
about these are the things that I'm
doing. They're not wholesale changes,
but these are the tools and resources
out there available that if you just
tweak a little bit and you add them into
what you're already doing, you're going
to see better results. Again,
specifically in math, I if you don't
master a skill and you move on to the
next skill, you're chances are you're
not going to master that skill as well.
That they're foundational and they build
on each other and and you know, in
education, we were good a long time of
just moving on. You know, we'll we'll
catch them up. It's you we don't have
that luxury anymore. and the
differentiation is broader and the uh
levels of ability are are widened. So,
anything that we can provide the
teachers that is valuable and can show
this is going to help student growth and
student achievement um because teachers
are good at heart. They want to help
kids and if there's tools out there like
that, they're going to gravitate to
them.
>> Thank you, S. Um we have over 600
district partners in the US and so many
more amazing things happening outside of
the US too with districts. What are you
seeing um districts bringing when it
really clicks when it really works when
we're seeing something that seems as
like on the moon as differentiation at
scale and like no really we figured it
out. What what are some what are you
seeing that's working?
Yeah, you know, uh, as you know, we have
a ton of efficacy studies on
kind of at what point does Khan Academy
really show powerful results and, uh,
you know, a lot of, you know, these
these thresholds are a little bit
arbitrary. The the researchers, and most
of these are third party researchers
that, you know, we talk to them, but
they do their own studies on us. Um it
you know they've looked at thresholds
like I mean even even if students do
nine hours a year of Khan Academy that's
not much that's I don't know like 20
minutes a week or 30 minutes a week it's
not much um you're already starting to
see a signal depending on the on the the
study you're looking at if you get to um
18 hours and also we've done some
studies as you know Vivve of our own
internal measures of of skills leveled
up and if you get you know roughly 18
hours and or 60 skills to proficient. Uh
that that yields pretty significant
gains on pretty much every interimm
assessment, state assessment we've we've
ever looked at. Um you know, a stud a
study just came out of India. I don't
know, you know, we're talking about the
US, but this one is powerful because if
we talk about underresourced school
districts, that's about as
underresourced as you could get. Um you
know, some of these kids aren't getting
meals or are wearing shoes and things
like that. And when um students got to a
reasonable level of dosage of Khan
Academy, you know, they the the
researchers saw a 0.44 effect size. And
for anyone who's, you know, familiar
with [snorts] any any kind of research,
but especially efficacy research in
education, even a a 2 effect size is
considered very very notable. And so
0.44 almost half a standard deviation.
Um, and you know, that's over seven
months. If these, to Phil's point, if
people keep doing it year after year,
uh, they're going to see dramatically,
uh, improved things. So, I say all of
that as as kind of the precursor to the
answer to your question, which is the
districts that are doing it very very
well are the ones that are able to drive
that motivation and that engagement. And
as Phil mentioned, it really is bringing
the teachers along. It's not um it's not
you I mean even if you wanted to force
we don't think you should but you're not
going to be able to um you know the best
examples we've seen are where there is
um
you know I mean I can think of several
examples where the person who's now
leading the Khan Academy implementation
it might be a chief academic officer it
might be a superintendent at the
district level oftentimes was a previous
user of Khan Academy in the classroom or
at a school level and what they do is
you know one make sure that we're there
to support and we being the district and
Khan Academy is there to support uh the
teachers run trainings etc. I mean, this
is something that I've learned. Um, you
know, even the districts that are
partnered with us and are sometimes, you
know, they're they're quote paying for
the training. You know, sometimes it's
hard to get that training in. You got to
do the training. And, you know, we
obviously do a lot of online learning
and I some say, well, we could do all
this asynchronous. No, it makes a huge
difference to your point, Phil, when
teachers are in the room, they can meet
our team and we can say like, look,
we're on the same side here. Um, we're
we we I mean like I've said very clear
as you know, Phil, like if I had to pick
between amazing teacher, amazing
technology, I'll pick amazing teacher
every time. My sister's a biology
teacher in New Orleans. And I'm like,
this is the best job in the AI. Like I
think it's going to be the safest job. I
think you're going to have a lot of, you
know, other people laid off and that
that are going to I think it's going to
be, you know, a lot of people are going
to want to become teachers. Um, uh,
which which I think is good good for
good for society. Uh but um the key is
is I think at the district level can you
provide soft accountability um carrots
more than sticks celebrate uh the
teachers that are doing great things you
know some of the stuff that we're
putting Aviv hinted this Khan Academy
reimagined which Phil y'all are piloting
you know we learned everything that we
saw over the last first two years Khan
Academy generally but also in K migo and
AI and we said okay how do we make it
more proactive active drive engagement.
We see a lot of the districts that do
the right things is they're keeping
track of where their teachers are.
They're not penalizing someone if they
haven't activated anything, but they're
doing some healthy competition. They
might have some leaderboards. They might
have some ways to engage folks. And
we're like, "Okay, can we productize
some of that?" Um, and make it easier
for a district official to make it fun
so that a teacher doesn't feel forced,
but they're like, "Oh, you know, so and
so down the hall is doing it and look,
their class is beating us. I want to I
want to win it." But it's it's fun. It's
not punitive. Uh those are the things
that we have seen can get districts and
there's some very large uh complex
districts that have now gotten to 60 70%
of their students are getting to those
18 hours a year, 60 skills to
proficient. And there, you know, these
aren't even efficacy studies now. um
Newark, New Jersey, uh their their state
test scores, they grew on state test
scores, not an efficacy, the northward
of New York that's been using it for a
couple years, they they saw twice the
average growth on state test scores, not
efficacy study, state test scores
district-wide. That includes even the
third the the 30% of classes that didn't
fully adopt yet. So, you know, if you
take the 70% that did, uh they're
they're they're seeing the results. And
and once again, there's no magic here. I
mean, there is magic, but the magic is
they're they're driving the practice.
They've prioritized it. They're
supporting the teachers who maybe
haven't engaged as much yet, and they're
really celebrating the ones that are
doing a good job.
>> Thank you.
>> Can I say something to that real quick?
>> I I I found it interesting that um the
the gems on the re-imagine. So, um we've
had a strong focus this year. SAT is a
required graduation assessment for our
juniors. So, it's important that they
they do their best. Um, so we we've been
using uh the SAT prep for Khan, but one
of the things that we've done is we put
groups of kids in the SA SRT classes,
their juniors, and then uh there was a
gems competition. And I thought, well,
that that would, you know, get
elementary, middle school. Our high
school kids were excited about
competing. like teachers were hearing
kids tell to the other kids, "Hey, you
better pull your weight. I want to win
this gym competition," which there's
nothing better than, you know, positive
uh reinforcement and motivation among
kids. Nowadays, that's half the battle
just to get them interested and engaged
because they're getting bombarded by so
many different things uh on their phone
alone. I mean, they're always bombarded
where wherever they are. So, I I thought
that was interesting and I loved I loved
hearing that.
>> Bill, you're talking about the gem
challenge we just launched like a week
or two ago.
>> Yeah. Yeah. Know, I'm super excited
about, you know, for any superintendents
listening, if you're not seeing it yet,
it's because you're not a pilot
district. And [laughter] so, that's
going to be mainstream uh back to
school, but this is actually some of the
first feedback I'm hearing. So, fingers
crossed. I'm glad people are looking at
it the right way. And since you
mentioned SAT, I'll also do a shameless
plug. Obviously, Khan Academy has always
been doing a ton for SAT, but sister
nonprofit to Khan Academy,
schoolhouse.world. It's now official
partner with the College Board for free
live tutoring on the SAT. And they do
that. The tutors are uh other high
school students who scored over a 700 on
a section. Um right now, my 17-year-old
is a tutor in math. So, some of your
kids might get and my 14-year-old, she
is a student in [laughter] ma in math on
on Schoolhouse. So, uh, people people
should also check that out.
>> That's wonderful. Thank you. Let's, um,
fill out that. You answered what I was
going to ask you about anyways, the last
question. So, great great job there. Um,
let's let's close here. I'm just going
to quickly share my screen so that for
folks for folks watching, you have an
easy way to get in touch with us. Um,
Khan Academy for your district. If you
are interested in seeing these gems, um,
if you want to talk about SAT or math or
writing, our team would love to speak
with you. You can scan this QR code.
It'll take you to our district's
website. Everyone will receive the
replay of this event. And, uh, I hope
that you'll join us for our next event
with Amy, Alzena, and S. Um, Phil, thank
you so much for your time. S, thank you
so much for your time. It was really
wonderful to spend the half hour with
you and I I wish you both a great rest
of your

transcript.full_text: Good morning, good afternoon, uh good evening depending on where you are. I am here with Dr. Phil Miko and Sal Khan. Um we're here for a superintendent fireside chat. Phil, you have agreed without really knowing to kick off this series. Uh S is going to be in conversation with other superintendent later this month. Uh if you happen to be at Spring Q powered by Cali, S will be in conversation with Amy Alzena from Cold Spring and Dr. Dr. Ruth Paris of LA County um with more to come. But let's just get into it. S you built Khan Academy from there was more to it than just this but essentially a YouTube channel into infrastructure that I don't you probably heard this news cell but we crossed over to 200 million learners just yesterday the other day. So that's pretty incredible. And you've spent the last few years thinking about what AI has to do with all of this. Phil, you're in the superintendency. you know what it costs politically, operationally to actually move a to actually move a district. So, I thought it'd be really interesting to get you two in conversation kind of leading in sort of different directions kind of working on the same thing. Um, my job in this conversation is to try to just keep us out of the clouds. If something gets too abstract, I'm bringing it back. >> No one's pointed at me. >> Yeah. So, let's just get into it. Phil, no warmup. Um, what is keeping you up right now? >> So, I just returned from a two-day finance workshop in Indie. I'm uh I'm in the state of Indiana, and what is really worrying me right now is funds for public education in our state. Um, specifically in our district, we have uh a really good highquality teachers and a and a I feel like a really good thing going. So, how do we continue to provide that quality with less money every year?
